checklist_item.title: Supporting Check: Can an attacker trigger division by zero through specific cell withholding patterns?

reachability.notes: The coset shift by PRIMITIVE_ROOT_OF_UNITY ensures Z(x) has no roots on the coset. If the shift is incorrect, division by zero crashes the node.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Analyze the recovery algorithm: which cells trigger which coset evaluations? 2. Determine if an attacker can choose which cells to withhold to influence which coset points are evaluated. 3. Check if any cell withholding pattern can cause zp_over_coset to evaluate to zero at specific indices. 4. Verify the 50% threshold check prevents attacks that require more than 50% cell withholding. 5. Test: systematically withhold different cell patterns and check if any cause division by zero. 6. Analyze: if PRIMITIVE_ROOT_OF_UNITY were incorrect, which cell patterns would expose the bug? 7. Check if the cell availability check prevents the attack before recovery is attempted. 8. Verify error handling: if division by zero occurs, does it crash the node or is it caught?
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-19, Trust Boundary: tb-003, Covered Node: actor-p2p-peer. Bug bounty notes: Requires PoC demonstrating division-by-zero crash via specific missing cell patterns.

checklist_item.title: Supporting Check: Does the validator node have defensive error handling for unexpected division by zero?

reachability.notes: The coset shift by PRIMITIVE_ROOT_OF_UNITY ensures Z(x) has no roots on the coset. If the shift is incorrect, division by zero crashes the node.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Examine the division operation implementation in recover_polynomialcoeff. 2. Check if there is defensive validation: verify zp_over_coset[i] != 0 before division. 3. If no explicit check: analyze error handling behavior in the field arithmetic library. 4. Verify: does division by zero throw an exception, return an error, or panic? 5. If exception/panic: does this propagate to crash the entire node or is it contained? 6. Check for any try/catch or error recovery around the recovery function. 7. Test: inject a zero value into zp_over_coset and observe behavior. 8. Recommend: add explicit zero checks with proper error handling as defense-in-depth.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-19, Trust Boundary: tb-003, Covered Node: actor-validator-node. Even if mathematically impossible, defensive checks prevent crashes from implementation bugs.

checklist_item.title: Invariant Falsification: Can coset_fft_field shift_factor deviate from PRIMITIVE_ROOT_OF_UNITY through any state transition or initialization path?

reachability.notes: This is a constant correctness property. An incorrect PRIMITIVE_ROOT_OF_UNITY would be a fundamental implementation bug affecting all coset-based operations.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Locate the coset_fft_field (SG-005) initialization code in the codebase. 2. Identify where shift_factor is assigned and verify it equals PRIMITIVE_ROOT_OF_UNITY. 3. Search for all code paths that could modify shift_factor after initialization. 4. Verify shift_factor is immutable or protected from modification. 5. Check for any configuration options, environment variables, or test overrides that could change shift_factor. 6. Examine any serialization/deserialization of coset_fft_field to ensure shift_factor is preserved correctly. 7. Test: inject an incorrect shift_factor value (e.g., 1 or another root of unity) and verify it causes division by zero in recover_polynomialcoeff. 8. Verify the invariant: mathematical proof that PRIMITIVE_ROOT_OF_UNITY^n \!= 1 for any divisor n of the FFT domain size. 9. Check for any edge cases in field initialization that could result in wrong values (e.g., integer overflow, incorrect modular arithmetic). 10. Verify unit tests exist that validate shift_factor equals PRIMITIVE_ROOT_OF_UNITY.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-20, Invariant: SG-005:INV-001, Primary Element: SG-005:coset_fft_field. This is an internal implementation correctness property. Incorrect shift_factor would cause systematic division-by-zero in all recovery operations.

checklist_item.title: Verify Trust Boundary Integrity for tb-001/tb-002/tb-003: Is the bit-reversal permutation (brp) correctly implemented as an involution in polynomial_eval_to_coeff, coset_for_cell, and coset_shift_for_cell?

reachability.notes: An incorrect bit-reversal permutation would produce wrong polynomial coefficients, leading to incorrect cells and proofs. This affects all blob processing and cell verification paths.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Locate the bit-reversal permutation implementation in the codebase (search for brp, bit_reversal, or similar functions). 2. Verify the mathematical correctness: for all valid indices i in the domain, brp(brp(i)) must equal i. 3. Review polynomial_eval_to_coeff (SG-007): check that brp is applied correctly to map evaluation-domain ordering to canonical FFT ordering. 4. Review coset_for_cell (SG-017): verify the brp application matches expected cell indexing. 5. Review coset_shift_for_cell (SG-016): confirm consistent use of brp for coset shift calculations. 6. Check edge cases: index 0, maximum index, domain size boundaries. 7. Verify the brp implementation handles the domain size correctly (should be power of 2). 8. Test: compute brp(brp(i)) for all i in [0, FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_BLOB) and verify equality to i. 9. Test: inject an incorrect brp (e.g., off-by-one) and verify it produces wrong polynomial coefficients. 10. Cross-reference: verify all three functions (SG-007, SG-016, SG-017) use the same brp implementation to avoid inconsistencies. 11. Check for any hardcoded lookup tables vs computed permutations and verify their correctness.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-21, Trust Boundaries: tb-001, tb-002, tb-003, Invariant: SG-007:INV-002, Covered Node: actor-validator-node. Incorrect bit-reversal permutation corrupts all polynomial conversions, producing incorrect cells and proofs. This is a fundamental cryptographic implementation bug that affects blob validation and cell verification.

checklist_item.title: Can division by zero polynomial be triggered via crafted missing cell indices in divide_polynomialcoeff?

reachability.notes: The divisor polynomial is the vanishing polynomial constructed from missing cell roots. If all cells are present (no missing roots), the vanishing polynomial is the constant 1, which is valid. However, edge cases in construction could produce a zero polynomial.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Locate the divide_polynomialcoeff function (SG-010) in the codebase. 2. Identify how the divisor polynomial b is constructed - it is the vanishing polynomial from missing cell roots. 3. Review the vanishing polynomial construction: verify it cannot produce a zero polynomial for any valid set of missing cell indices. 4. Test edge cases: (a) zero missing cells (should produce constant polynomial 1), (b) all cells missing, (c) boundary indices, (d) duplicate indices. 5. Verify the pre-condition check: confirm the function validates that b is not the zero polynomial before division. 6. Verify the leading coefficient check: confirm b[bpos] is validated to be nonzero. 7. Attempt to craft a set of missing cell indices that would produce roots evaluating to zero at all points. 8. Test: inject a zero polynomial as divisor and verify the function rejects it with appropriate error. 9. Test: inject a polynomial with zero leading coefficient and verify rejection. 10. Check for integer overflow/underflow in bpos calculation that could lead to accessing wrong coefficient. 11. Verify error handling: confirm division-by-zero attempts do not crash the node but return proper errors.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-22, Trust Boundary: tb-003, Pre-condition: SG-010:INV-001, Assumption: asm-007, Covered Node: actor-validator-node. Division by zero polynomial crashes the node during proof generation or polynomial recovery. Requires PoC showing a set of missing cell indices that produces a zero vanishing polynomial.

checklist_item.title: Verify Trust Boundary Integrity for tb-002/tb-003: Are all xs values validated to be distinct before Lagrange interpolation in interpolate_polynomialcoeff?

reachability.notes: The xs values are coset points derived from cell_indices. If duplicate cell_indices slip through validation, duplicate coset points would be passed to interpolation, causing a crash.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Locate the interpolate_polynomialcoeff function (SG-011) in the codebase. 2. Identify how the xs array is constructed - these are coset points derived from cell_indices. 3. CRITICAL: Verify that cell_indices are validated for uniqueness BEFORE coset point computation. 4. Trace the data flow: P2P message -> cell_indices extraction -> coset point computation -> interpolation call. 5. Review cell index validation layer: confirm duplicates are explicitly rejected with appropriate error. 6. Review coset computation layer: check if duplicate detection exists as a secondary defense. 7. Attempt to bypass validation: craft a P2P message with duplicate cell_indices and observe behavior. 8. Test edge cases: (a) adjacent duplicate indices, (b) non-adjacent duplicates, (c) all same index, (d) maximum valid indices with one duplicate. 9. Verify the (xs[i] - xs[j]).inverse() operation: confirm it uses checked arithmetic or has explicit zero-check before inversion. 10. Test: inject duplicate coset points directly and verify the function either rejects them or the inverse operation fails safely without crashing. 11. Check error handling: confirm that division-by-zero in Lagrange interpolation does not crash the node but returns a proper error. 12. Verify the invariant: for all i != j, xs[i] != xs[j] must hold at function entry. 13. Cross-reference with cell availability logic: ensure the 50% threshold logic cannot produce duplicate indices.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-23, Trust Boundaries: tb-002, tb-003, Pre-condition: SG-011:INV-001, Assumption: asm-011, Covered Nodes: actor-p2p-peer, actor-validator-node. Duplicate xs values in Lagrange interpolation cause division by zero when computing (xs[i] - xs[j]).inverse(). This is a single-node DoS vulnerability reachable via crafted P2P messages. Requires PoC showing a crash in interpolation triggered by duplicate cell_indices that bypass validation.

checklist_item.title: Verify Trust Boundary Integrity for tb-001/tb-003: Is len(a) + len(b) <= FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_EXT_BLOB validated before polynomial multiplication in multiply_polynomialcoeff?

reachability.notes: The assertion prevents buffer overflow during polynomial multiplication. If many cells are missing (close to 50%), the vanishing polynomial can be large, and the product with another polynomial could exceed bounds.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Locate the multiply_polynomialcoeff function (SG-009) in the codebase. 2. Identify all callers of this function and trace input polynomials back to their sources. 3. CRITICAL: Verify the pre-condition len(a) + len(b) <= FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_EXT_BLOB is explicitly checked before multiplication. 4. Trace polynomial a: identify its origin (typically the data polynomial from blob or recovered polynomial). 5. Trace polynomial b: identify its origin (typically the vanishing polynomial from missing cell roots). 6. Analyze vanishing polynomial size: when close to 50% of cells are missing, the vanishing polynomial has ~CELLS_PER_EXT_BLOB/2 roots, resulting in degree ~CELLS_PER_EXT_BLOB/2. 7. Calculate worst case: if data polynomial has degree FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_BLOB and vanishing polynomial has maximum degree, verify the product cannot exceed FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_EXT_BLOB. 8. Test edge cases: (a) exactly 50% cells missing, (b) just under 50% missing, (c) minimum cells present for recovery. 9. Test: craft a recovery request with a specific pattern of missing cells that maximizes vanishing polynomial degree and attempt multiplication. 10. Verify error handling: if bounds are exceeded, confirm the function rejects the operation with appropriate error rather than producing incorrect FFT results. 11. Check for buffer overflow: in memory-unsafe languages, verify array allocations account for maximum product degree. 12. Verify FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_EXT_BLOB constant is correctly defined relative to FFT domain size. 13. Cross-reference with cell availability threshold logic: ensure the 50% threshold mathematically guarantees product bounds.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-24, Trust Boundaries: tb-001, tb-003, Pre-condition: SG-009:INV-001, Assumption: asm-010, Primary Element: SG-009:multiply_polynomialcoeff. Exceeding the extended evaluation domain during polynomial multiplication causes incorrect FFT results, potentially leading to invalid proofs or corrupted blob recovery. In memory-unsafe languages, this could also cause buffer overflow. Requires PoC with specific missing cell patterns that maximize vanishing polynomial degree.

checklist_item.title: Verify Trust Boundary Integrity for tb-002: Is cell_index validated to be in [0, CELLS_PER_EXT_BLOB) before array access in coset_shift_for_cell?

reachability.notes: Attacker-controlled cell_index from P2P input. FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_CELL * cell_index could overflow or exceed array bounds.

checklist_item.test_procedure:
1. Locate the coset_shift_for_cell function (SG-016) in the codebase.
2. Identify the assertion or validation check for cell_index bounds: cell_index MUST be in [0, CELLS_PER_EXT_BLOB).
3. CRITICAL: Verify this bounds check occurs BEFORE the array access roots_brp[FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_CELL * cell_index].
4. Trace cell_index origin: P2P message -> cell_index extraction -> coset_shift_for_cell call.
5. Review the P2P gossip validation layer: confirm cell_index is validated at the boundary before reaching this function.
6. Check for integer overflow: FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_CELL * cell_index could overflow if cell_index is large. Verify multiplication uses checked arithmetic or the bounds check prevents overflow.
7. Test: inject cell_index = CELLS_PER_EXT_BLOB (just out of bounds) and verify rejection with appropriate error.
8. Test: inject cell_index = UINT64_MAX (or maximum value for the type) and verify safe rejection without overflow.
9. Test: inject cell_index = -1 (if signed type) and verify bounds check catches it.
10. Verify array bounds: confirm roots_brp array has exactly FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_EXT_BLOB elements, making max valid index FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_CELL * (CELLS_PER_EXT_BLOB - 1) + FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_CELL - 1.
11. Check error handling: confirm out-of-bounds access attempts result in proper error response, not crash or undefined behavior.
12. Verify the function signature: if cell_index uses unsigned type, negative values are not possible but large values still could overflow.
13. Cross-reference with caller functions: ensure all callers validate cell_index before calling coset_shift_for_cell.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-25, Trust Boundary: tb-002, Pre-condition: SG-016:INV-001, Threat: stride-017, Primary Element: SG-016:coset_shift_for_cell. Out-of-bounds array access via crafted cell_index from P2P input can crash the node or cause memory corruption. Requires PoC showing node crash from crafted cell_index.

checklist_item.title: Verify Trust Boundary Integrity for tb-002: Is cell_index validated to be in [0, CELLS_PER_EXT_BLOB) and slice bounds checked in coset_for_cell?

reachability.notes: Same as PROP-0025 but for the full coset retrieval.

checklist_item.test_procedure:
1. Locate the coset_for_cell function (SG-017) in the codebase.
2. Identify the assertion or validation check for cell_index bounds: cell_index MUST be in [0, CELLS_PER_EXT_BLOB).
3. CRITICAL: Verify this bounds check occurs BEFORE the slice operation roots_brp[start : start + FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_CELL].
4. Calculate start index: start = FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_CELL * cell_index.
5. Verify start calculation uses checked arithmetic to prevent integer overflow.
6. Verify slice bounds: start + FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_CELL <= len(roots_brp) which equals FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_EXT_BLOB.
7. Trace cell_index origin: P2P message -> cell_index extraction -> coset_for_cell call.
8. Review the P2P gossip validation layer: confirm cell_index is validated at the boundary before reaching this function.
9. Test: inject cell_index = CELLS_PER_EXT_BLOB (just out of bounds) and verify rejection with appropriate error.
10. Test: inject cell_index = UINT64_MAX (or maximum value for the type) and verify safe rejection without overflow in start calculation.
11. Test: verify the returned Coset has exactly FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_CELL elements (not more, not fewer).
12. Test: compare with coset_shift_for_cell (SG-016) - both functions should have consistent bounds checking.
13. Check error handling: confirm out-of-bounds slice access attempts result in proper error response, not crash or undefined behavior.
14. Verify the relationship: this function returns a full Coset array while coset_shift_for_cell returns a single element - ensure consistent validation.
15. Cross-reference with caller functions: ensure all callers validate cell_index before calling coset_for_cell.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-26, Trust Boundary: tb-002, Pre-condition: SG-017:INV-001, Threat: stride-017, Primary Element: SG-017:coset_for_cell, Covered Nodes: actor-p2p-peer, actor-validator-node. Out-of-bounds slice access via crafted cell_index from P2P input can crash the node or return incorrect coset elements leading to proof generation failures. This is a companion check to CHK-0158 (coset_shift_for_cell) - both functions access roots_brp with cell_index and must have consistent validation. Requires PoC showing node crash or incorrect behavior from crafted cell_index.

checklist_item.title: Verify Trust Boundary Integrity for tb-002: Does cell_to_coset_evals produce exactly FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_CELL elements and validate byte segment sizes?

reachability.notes: Cells received via P2P are deserialized. If the cell is shorter than expected, byte slicing produces undersized field element bytes.

checklist_item.test_procedure:
1. Locate the cell_to_coset_evals function (SG-001) in the codebase.
2. Verify the function signature and return type: it MUST return a CosetEvals with exactly FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_CELL elements.
3. CRITICAL: Trace the input cell bytes from P2P message deserialization to this function.
4. Identify the byte slicing logic: each segment [i*BYTES_PER_FIELD_ELEMENT : (i+1)*BYTES_PER_FIELD_ELEMENT] must extract exactly BYTES_PER_FIELD_ELEMENT bytes.
5. Test undersized cell input: craft a P2P message with a cell shorter than expected (less than FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_CELL * BYTES_PER_FIELD_ELEMENT bytes).
6. Verify behavior with undersized cells: (a) check if the function detects the short input, (b) confirm it rejects with appropriate error rather than producing undersized field elements.
7. Test edge cases: (a) cell exactly one byte short, (b) cell missing one complete field element, (c) empty cell, (d) cell with extra trailing bytes.
8. Check the Cell byte length check validation layer: verify it executes BEFORE cell_to_coset_evals is called.
9. Test: bypass or disable the length check and verify cell_to_coset_evals has secondary defense.
10. Verify CosetEvals output invariant: confirm the returned array always has exactly FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_CELL elements, no more, no fewer.
11. Check for panic/crash conditions: if byte slicing encounters insufficient data, does it panic or return error?
12. Verify field element deserialization: each BYTES_PER_FIELD_ELEMENT byte segment must be valid field element bytes.
13. Test: inject invalid field element bytes (e.g., value >= field modulus) and verify proper rejection.
14. Cross-reference with cell verification logic: ensure cells pass integrity checks before reaching this function.
15. Verify memory safety: in memory-unsafe languages, confirm no buffer over-read when cell is undersized.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-27, Trust Boundary: tb-002, Invariant: SG-001:INV-001, Primary Element: SG-001:cell_to_coset_evals, Covered Nodes: actor-p2p-peer, actor-validator-node. Undersized cell bytes from P2P input could produce incorrect coset evaluations if byte slicing is not properly validated. This leads to verification errors and potential consensus issues. Requires PoC with malformed cell bytes.

checklist_item.title: Falsification Test: Can the roundtrip invariant coset_evals_to_cell(cell_to_coset_evals(cell)) == cell be violated for valid field elements?

reachability.notes: Roundtrip property. If violated, recovered cells differ from originals even with correct polynomial.

checklist_item.test_procedure:
1. Locate both functions: cell_to_coset_evals (SG-001) and coset_evals_to_cell (SG-002) in the codebase.
2. Understand the transformation: cell_to_coset_evals converts a Cell (byte array) into CosetEvals (array of field elements), and coset_evals_to_cell reverses this.
3. CRITICAL: Design a falsification test - attempt to find a valid cell input where the roundtrip produces a different result.
4. Test with canonical cell values: create cells with various valid field element byte representations and verify roundtrip equality.
5. Test boundary field elements: (a) zero element (all zero bytes), (b) one element, (c) maximum valid field element (modulus - 1), (d) elements near modulus boundary.
6. Test structured patterns: (a) alternating bytes, (b) repeated patterns, (c) cells derived from actual blob data.
7. Verify byte-level equality: the comparison must be exact byte equality, not just field element equality.
8. Check for serialization/deserialization asymmetry: ensure the byte representation used by coset_evals_to_cell matches what cell_to_coset_evals expects.
9. Check for normalization issues: if field elements can have multiple byte representations, verify consistent normalization.
10. Trace through the codebase: identify where this roundtrip is relied upon in the cell recovery/reconstruction flow.
11. Test with recovered cells: after recover_cells_and_kzg_proofs, verify that reconstructed cells pass the roundtrip test.
12. Check endianness: verify both functions use consistent byte ordering (big-endian vs little-endian).
13. Verify FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_CELL alignment: ensure both functions process exactly the same number of elements.
14. Property-based testing: if available, use fuzzing to generate random valid cells and verify roundtrip holds.
15. Impact analysis: if violated, recovered cells would differ from original cells even with a correct polynomial, causing invalid sidecars and potential consensus failures.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-28, Invariant: SG-002:INV-002, Primary Element: SG-002:coset_evals_to_cell, Covered Nodes: actor-validator-node. This is a fundamental correctness property for cell serialization. Violation would cause recovered cells to differ from originals, producing invalid blob sidecars. While classified as internal-only, the impact propagates to consensus if sidecars fail verification. The test should attempt to FALSIFY the invariant by finding edge cases where roundtrip fails.

checklist_item.title: Falsification Test: Can _fft_field accept non-power-of-2 input lengths or mismatched vals/roots_of_unity lengths?

reachability.notes: FFT requires power-of-2 sizes for correctness. Non-power-of-2 sizes cause incorrect butterfly operations.

checklist_item.test_procedure:
1. Locate the _fft_field function (SG-003) in the codebase.
2. Identify the pre-condition check: len(vals) MUST be a power of 2 AND len(vals) MUST equal len(roots_of_unity).
3. CRITICAL: Verify these checks occur BEFORE any FFT butterfly operations begin.
4. Design falsification tests for non-power-of-2 lengths:
   (a) len(vals) = 3 (prime, not power of 2)
   (b) len(vals) = 5 (prime, not power of 2)
   (c) len(vals) = 6 (composite, not power of 2)
   (d) len(vals) = 7 (prime, not power of 2)
   (e) len(vals) = 9 (composite, not power of 2)
   (f) len(vals) = 15 (one less than power of 2)
   (g) len(vals) = 17 (one more than power of 2)
5. Design falsification tests for mismatched lengths:
   (a) len(vals) = 8, len(roots_of_unity) = 16 (roots larger)
   (b) len(vals) = 16, len(roots_of_unity) = 8 (vals larger)
   (c) len(vals) = 8, len(roots_of_unity) = 7 (both different, neither power of 2)
6. Verify rejection behavior: each invalid input must be rejected with an appropriate error, NOT silently accepted.
7. Check for silent corruption: if validation is missing, FFT butterfly operations on non-power-of-2 arrays produce incorrect results.
8. Trace callers: identify all functions that call _fft_field and verify they always provide power-of-2 sized arrays.
9. Check roots_of_unity source: verify roots_of_unity is always generated with power-of-2 length matching vals.
10. Test edge cases for valid power-of-2 lengths:
   (a) len(vals) = 1 (2^0)
   (b) len(vals) = 2 (2^1)
   (c) len(vals) = 4096 (typical EIP-4844 value)
   (d) len(vals) = 8192 (next power of 2)
11. Verify is_power_of_2 check implementation: common patterns include (n & (n-1)) == 0 for n > 0.
12. Check for off-by-one: ensure len(vals) = 0 is handled (0 is technically power of 2 by some definitions but may be invalid).
13. Impact analysis: if non-power-of-2 inputs are accepted, butterfly indices would access wrong elements, producing garbage polynomial evaluations.
14. Cross-reference with polynomial commitment scheme: FFT outputs feed into KZG commitment generation - corrupted FFT breaks all downstream proofs.
15. Verify the invariant SG-003:INV-001 is enforced: the function must guarantee power-of-2 constraint holds for all accepted inputs.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-29, Invariant: SG-003:INV-001, Primary Element: SG-003:_fft_field, Covered Nodes: actor-validator-node. FFT correctness fundamentally requires power-of-2 array sizes. Non-power-of-2 inputs cause the butterfly operation indices to access incorrect elements, propagating errors to all polynomial evaluations, cell generation, and KZG proofs. While internal-only, a bug here would corrupt the entire cryptographic pipeline. The test should attempt to FALSIFY the pre-condition by finding code paths that bypass the power-of-2 check.

checklist_item.title: Falsification Test: Can the inverse FFT normalization factor 1/N be bypassed or computed incorrectly, causing ifft(fft(p)) != p?

reachability.notes: The inverse FFT is used in polynomial_eval_to_coeff and recover_polynomialcoeff. Incorrect normalization produces wrong coefficients.

checklist_item.test_procedure:
1. Locate the fft_field function (SG-004) and its inverse FFT implementation in the codebase.
2. Identify the normalization step: inverse FFT MUST multiply by 1/N where N is the array length.
3. CRITICAL: Verify the normalization factor is computed as the modular multiplicative inverse of N in the field, not integer division.
4. Design falsification tests for the roundtrip invariant ifft(fft(p)) == p:
   (a) Test with the zero polynomial (all zero coefficients)
   (b) Test with the one polynomial (constant term = 1, rest zeros)
   (c) Test with a polynomial where all coefficients = 1
   (d) Test with random coefficient values
   (e) Test with maximum field element values (modulus - 1)
   (f) Test with polynomial degree = N-1 (maximum degree)
5. Verify roots_of_unity order reversal: inverse FFT MUST reverse the roots_of_unity order (except the first element).
   (a) For forward FFT: use roots_of_unity as [1, w, w^2, ..., w^(N-1)]
   (b) For inverse FFT: use roots_of_unity as [1, w^(N-1), w^(N-2), ..., w^1]
   (c) Verify the implementation correctly swaps or reverses the roots.
6. Test normalization edge cases:
   (a) N = 1: normalization factor should be 1 (1/1 = 1)
   (b) N = 2: verify 1/2 mod field_modulus is correctly computed
   (c) N = 4096 (typical EIP-4844 value): verify 1/4096 mod field_modulus
   (d) N = field_modulus - 1: edge case for modular inverse
7. Trace the normalization implementation:
   (a) Check if normalization is applied element-wise after butterfly operations
   (b) Verify normalization is not accidentally applied in forward FFT
   (c) Check for off-by-one errors in normalization loop
8. Test polynomial_eval_to_coeff and recover_polynomialcoeff:
   (a) These functions use inverse FFT - verify they receive correctly normalized output
   (b) Test with known polynomial coefficients, compute evaluations, then recover coefficients
   (c) Verify recovered coefficients exactly match original coefficients
9. Check for precision/rounding issues: field arithmetic should be exact with no floating point
10. Verify modular inverse computation:
   (a) Check the algorithm used (extended Euclidean algorithm, Fermat's little theorem, etc.)
   (b) Verify it handles edge cases correctly
   (c) Confirm result satisfies: (N * (1/N)) mod field_modulus == 1
11. Cross-reference with KZG commitment verification:
   (a) Incorrect FFT normalization would produce wrong polynomial coefficients
   (b) This would cause commitment mismatches and proof verification failures
12. Impact analysis: if normalization is wrong by any constant factor, all polynomial coefficient computations are corrupted, breaking proofs and data recovery.
13. Property-based testing: generate random polynomials and verify ifft(fft(p)) == p holds for all.
14. Compare with reference implementation: if available, compare FFT outputs against a known-correct implementation.
15. Verify the invariant SG-004:INV-001 is enforced across all code paths that use fft_field for inverse FFT.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-30, Invariant: SG-004:INV-001, Primary Element: SG-004:fft_field, Covered Nodes: actor-validator-node. The inverse FFT normalization factor 1/N is critical for polynomial coefficient recovery. If normalization is missing, incorrect, or applied inconsistently, all polynomial operations downstream are corrupted. This affects polynomial_eval_to_coeff and recover_polynomialcoeff, which are essential for cell recovery and KZG proof generation. The roots_of_unity order reversal (except first element) is equally critical - incorrect ordering produces garbage outputs. While classified as internal-only, corruption propagates to all cryptographic proofs. The test should attempt to FALSIFY the roundtrip invariant by finding edge cases where ifft(fft(p)) != p.

checklist_item.title: Verify Trust Boundary Integrity for tb-001: Does compute_cells_and_kzg_proofs strictly enforce BYTES_PER_BLOB input size and produce exactly CELLS_PER_EXT_BLOB cells and proofs?

reachability.notes: A blob submitter provides blob bytes via type-3 transaction. Incorrect blob size must be rejected before polynomial operations.

checklist_item.test_procedure:
1. Locate the compute_cells_and_kzg_proofs function (SG-020) in the codebase.
2. Identify the input validation check for blob size: MUST be exactly BYTES_PER_BLOB (131072 bytes for EIP-4844).
3. CRITICAL: Verify this size check occurs BEFORE any polynomial conversion or cell computation begins.
4. Design boundary crossing tests for undersized blobs:
   (a) blob size = 0 bytes (empty blob)
   (b) blob size = 1 byte (minimal non-empty)
   (c) blob size = BYTES_PER_BLOB - 1 (off-by-one short)
   (d) blob size = BYTES_PER_BLOB / 2 (half size)
   (e) blob size = 31 bytes (one field element minus one byte)
5. Design boundary crossing tests for oversized blobs:
   (a) blob size = BYTES_PER_BLOB + 1 (off-by-one long)
   (b) blob size = BYTES_PER_BLOB + 32 (one extra field element)
   (c) blob size = BYTES_PER_BLOB * 2 (double size)
   (d) blob size = MAX_UINT64 (extreme overflow attempt)
6. Verify output cardinality invariant:
   (a) Check that CELLS_PER_EXT_BLOB cells are always produced for valid input
   (b) Check that CELLS_PER_EXT_BLOB proofs are always produced for valid input
   (c) Verify cells array length == proofs array length
   (d) For EIP-4844/Fusaka: CELLS_PER_EXT_BLOB = 128
7. Test the validation layer ordering:
   (a) Does transaction validation check blob size first?
   (b) If transaction validation is bypassed (e.g., via internal API), does compute_cells_and_kzg_proofs still validate?
   (c) Trace call paths from transaction ingestion to compute_cells_and_kzg_proofs
8. Attack vector analysis:
   (a) Can an attacker craft a type-3 transaction with malformed blob size?
   (b) Can RLP encoding tricks bypass length validation?
   (c) Are there any blob size fields that could be inconsistent (declared vs actual)?
9. Impact assessment if validation is missing:
   (a) Undersized blob: polynomial conversion would read garbage or crash on out-of-bounds access
   (b) Oversized blob: extra bytes ignored OR buffer overflow in polynomial conversion
   (c) Wrong cell count: downstream consumers (validators, data availability sampling) would receive corrupted data
10. Cross-reference with blob_to_polynomial (SG-018):
    (a) Verify SG-018 also enforces BYTES_PER_BLOB constraint
    (b) Check for defense-in-depth: multiple validation layers
11. Verify error handling:
    (a) Invalid blob size MUST return a clear error, not crash
    (b) Error must be deterministic across implementations
    (c) Check for panic/exception paths that could cause node denial-of-service
12. Check CELLS_PER_EXT_BLOB constant usage:
    (a) Is it hardcoded or derived from FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_EXT_BLOB / FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_CELL?
    (b) Verify consistency across all references
    (c) For Fusaka: CELLS_PER_EXT_BLOB = 128 (8192 / 64)
13. Fuzz testing recommendation:
    (a) Generate random blob sizes near BYTES_PER_BLOB boundary
    (b) Generate blobs with valid size but corrupted content
    (c) Test concurrent blob submissions with mixed valid/invalid sizes
14. Verify the pre-condition from invariant SG-020:INV-002 is enforced at the trust boundary (tb-001) before any processing occurs.
15. PoC requirement: demonstrate a blob that bypasses size validation and causes incorrect polynomial representation or cell count.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-31, Invariant: SG-020:INV-002, Trust Boundary: tb-001, Primary Element: SG-020:compute_cells_and_kzg_proofs, Covered Nodes: actor-blob-submitter, actor-block-proposer. This is a BOUNDARY PROPERTY where external blob submitters provide untrusted data via type-3 transactions. The validation must occur at the trust boundary before polynomial operations begin. Two critical invariants: (1) input blob MUST be exactly BYTES_PER_BLOB, (2) output MUST contain exactly CELLS_PER_EXT_BLOB cells and proofs. A successful bypass could cause incorrect polynomial representations, leading to invalid KZG proofs or data availability sampling failures. Bug bounty eligible - requires PoC demonstrating size validation bypass.

checklist_item.title: Verify Trust Boundary Integrity for tb-001: Are blocks with non-empty ommers properly rejected at the P2P boundary in post-merge Osaka fork?

reachability.notes: Attacker includes non-empty ommers list in a post-merge block. If not validated, this violates the PoS invariant.

checklist_item.test_procedure:
1. Identify the block validation entry point in the P2P message handler where incoming blocks are deserialized.
2. Craft a post-merge (Osaka fork) block with a non-empty ommers list in the block body.
3. Submit the malformed block via P2P network protocol and observe the validation response.
4. Verify the block is rejected with an appropriate error code before further processing.
5. Confirm the ommers_hash header field is validated to equal EMPTY_OMMER_HASH.
6. Check that no partial state changes occur from processing the invalid block.
7. Review error handling to ensure no information leakage about internal validation logic.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W7B16-16, Trust Boundary: tb-001, Invariant: SG-004/INV-004, Threat: stride-010

checklist_item.title: Does actor-p2p-peer correctly validate ommers_hash equals EMPTY_OMMER_HASH before forwarding blocks?

reachability.notes: Attacker includes non-empty ommers list in a post-merge block. If not validated, this violates the PoS invariant.

checklist_item.test_procedure:
1. Locate the P2P peer block reception handler that processes incoming block headers.
2. Trace the code path that validates the ommers_hash field against EMPTY_OMMER_HASH constant.
3. Verify validation occurs before the block is forwarded to other peers or the execution engine.
4. Test with a block where ommers_hash does not equal EMPTY_OMMER_HASH but ommers list is empty.
5. Test with a block where ommers_hash equals EMPTY_OMMER_HASH but ommers list is non-empty.
6. Confirm both inconsistent cases are rejected.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W7B16-16, Node: actor-p2p-peer, Supporting boundary security for tb-001

checklist_item.title: Does actor-execution-engine enforce the empty ommers invariant during block execution?

reachability.notes: Attacker includes non-empty ommers list in a post-merge block. If not validated, this violates the PoS invariant.

checklist_item.test_procedure:
1. Identify the execution engine's block validation function that processes block bodies.
2. Verify the ommers tuple emptiness check is present and correctly implemented.
3. Test block execution with a crafted block containing non-empty ommers that bypassed P2P validation.
4. Confirm the execution engine independently validates ommers are empty as a defense-in-depth measure.
5. Verify consensus-critical state is not modified before ommers validation completes.
6. Check for any code paths that could skip ommers validation during state sync or snap sync.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W7B16-16, Node: actor-execution-engine, Supporting boundary security for tb-001

checklist_item.title: Verify Trust Boundary Integrity: Does the trie root() function produce deterministic, order-independent 32-byte roots regardless of key insertion order?
checklist_item.test_procedure:
1. Create a trie and insert a set of key-value pairs in order A, compute root.
2. Create a fresh trie and insert the same key-value pairs in order B (reverse or random), compute root.
3. Verify both roots are identical (bit-for-bit comparison).
4. Repeat with various key-value sets including edge cases (empty values, max-length keys).
5. Test with concurrent insertions if applicable to verify thread-safety doesn't affect determinism.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-0001, Trust Boundary: tb-006, Invariant: INV-020. Consensus-critical property.

checklist_item.title: Verify Trust Boundary Integrity: Does the patricialize function guarantee termination with strictly increasing level parameter in all recursive calls?
checklist_item.test_procedure:
1. Instrument patricialize to log the 'level' parameter on each recursive call.
2. Submit transactions creating pathological key sets (all same prefix, incrementing by 1 nibble).
3. Verify level strictly increases on each recursive call (level_recursive > level_caller).
4. Confirm maximum recursion depth is bounded by max_nibble_length (64 for hashed keys).
5. Test with both secured (hashed) and unsecured tries to verify termination in both modes.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-0002, Trust Boundary: tb-001, tb-008, Invariant: INV-028. Infinite recursion = node crash.

checklist_item.title: Can the trie._data dictionary contain entries with default values (ghost entries) after trie_set operations?
checklist_item.test_procedure:
1. Create a trie and set key K to a non-default value V.
2. Set key K to trie.default (deletion).
3. Inspect trie._data directly to verify K is absent (not present with default value).
4. Compute root and compare against expected empty trie root.
5. Test edge cases: setting to default when key doesn't exist, rapid set/delete cycles.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-0003, Invariant: INV-006. Ghost entries corrupt Merkle root calculation.

checklist_item.title: Does trie_get correctly return the value set by trie_set, and return trie.default for unset keys?
checklist_item.test_procedure:
1. Create empty trie, verify trie_get(any_key) returns trie.default.
2. Set key K to value V using trie_set.
3. Verify trie_get(K) returns exactly V (identity check, not equality).
4. Test with various value types (Account, Bytes, U256, etc.).
5. Test overwrite: set K to V1, then to V2, verify trie_get returns V2.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-0004, Invariant: INV-008. Fundamental trie correctness property.

checklist_item.title: Verify Trust Boundary Integrity: Are all keys in secured tries properly hashed with keccak256 before storage in the prepared trie mapping?
checklist_item.test_procedure:
1. Create a secured trie (trie.secured = True) and insert key-value pairs.
2. Examine the prepared trie mapping output from _prepare_trie.
3. Verify each key in the mapping is bytes_to_nibble_list(keccak256(original_key)).
4. Confirm no raw (unhashed) keys appear in the mapping.
5. Attempt to insert a key via code path that might bypass hashing and verify it fails or is hashed.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-0005, Trust Boundary: tb-007, Invariant: INV-018. Key hashing prevents trie-balancing attacks.

checklist_item.title: Can encode_node produce empty bytes (b'') for any valid input, violating the non-empty encoding invariant?
checklist_item.test_procedure:
1. Review encode_node for all type dispatch branches.
2. For each supported type, craft edge-case inputs that could produce empty encoding.
3. Test with empty byte strings, zero values, empty accounts, etc.
4. Verify assertion exists in _prepare_trie checking encoded_value != b''.
5. Confirm assertion triggers (or handling occurs) if empty encoding is detected.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-0006, Invariant: INV-017. Empty encoded values corrupt trie structure.

checklist_item.title: Verify Trust Boundary Integrity: Does encode_node for Account values always receive and use a valid (non-None) storage root from get_storage_root callback?
checklist_item.test_procedure:
1. Create an Account with no storage (empty storage trie).
2. Trace the encode_node call to verify get_storage_root is invoked.
3. Verify callback returns EMPTY_TRIE_ROOT (not None) for empty storage.
4. Test with newly created accounts, accounts with cleared storage.
5. Attempt to invoke encode_node(account, storage_root=None) directly and verify it raises.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-0007, Trust Boundary: tb-002, Invariant: INV-003. Missing storage root = corrupted account encoding.

checklist_item.title: Verify Trust Boundary Integrity: Does get_storage_root callback return the correct, canonical 32-byte storage root for each address?
checklist_item.test_procedure:
1. Create an account with known storage entries.
2. Compute expected storage root independently using verified implementation.
3. Invoke get_storage_root for the address and compare against expected.
4. Test edge cases: empty storage, single entry, max entries, special key patterns.
5. Verify storage root changes correctly after storage modifications.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-0008, Trust Boundary: tb-002. Silent storage root corruption = consensus split.
